---PLAN---
# Planetfall — Plan & Status

_Last updated: 2026-06-05_

## The pitch

You're a stranded astronaut. Your ship's AI has **amnesia** — it lost all its
context about what happened. To escape, you explore a 3D planet, find debris,
and each piece hides a small puzzle solved by **recovering lost context**.

The hook: reconstructing the ship's memory in-fiction **is the same act** as
recovering a real codebase's history with [Entire](https://docs.entire.io).
Each puzzle teaches a real `entire` command.

> Note: `entire explain` does **not** exist (it was an early guess). The real
> context-recovery commands are `recap`, `checkpoint`, `activity`, `dispatch`.

## Locked decisions

- **3D, web-based.** Three.js + Vite, no downloaded assets — the planet is
  generated procedurally at load.
- **Planet look (approved):** a dreamy **lavender ocean world** with a few
  **gold metallic islands** that shimmer, wrapped in a **Saturn-style ring**.
  Soft, minimal, calm.
  - NOT earthlike. NOT the glowing-magenta-"neural"-vein look (tried it; it
    looked "itchy" — busy land texture fighting bright veins. Removed.)
  - Keep lighting (sun / ambient / env) **low** — the lavender sea washes out
    white if over-lit.
- **Build order:** get the world feeling right **first** (done), then puzzles,
  then the command engine + win/lose.

## Status

### Done ✅ — the 3D world (user-approved)
- Procedural planet: lavender ocean, gold **metallic** islands (PBR
  metalness + low roughness + `RoomEnvironment` IBL → shimmer), soft frost
  poles, drifting clouds.
- Saturn-style banded ring (tilted, two gaps, transparent).
- Soft lavender fresnel atmosphere, violet-tinted starfield.
- Orbit + zoom controls; slow auto-rotation.
- 4 clickable debris on the surface with hover highlight; clicking opens a
  **stub** fragment panel (lore text + the command it will teach).
- Verified rendering in headless Chrome (WebGL2), no console errors.

### Not built yet ⏳
- Puzzle mechanics (panels are lore stubs only).
- The command engine (see open questions).
- Win/lose + checkpoint-collection state.
- Polish backlog: bloom on gold glints, ring shadow cast on planet, recolor
  debris halos from cyan → warm gold/white to match the palette, spread the
  islands out a bit.

## Puzzle design (PROPOSED — not yet reviewed/built)

Arc: **orient → restore → understand → escape**. Each puzzle is small: run a
command → read the recovered context → use one detail from it.

| # | Fragment | Command | What's lost | Puzzle |
|---|----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | 🪖 EVA Helmet | `recap` | "What was I doing before the crash?" | Run `recap`; read the summary of recent log entries; pick out the mission. |
| 2 | 📖 Torn Logbook | `checkpoint` | A setting got corrupted in the crash | Search checkpoints, find the last good one before the corruption, rewind to it. |
| 3 | 🖥️ Nav Panel | `activity` | "*When* did it go wrong?" | Read the activity timeline; find when the anomaly started. |
| 4 | 📡 Signal Beacon | `dispatch` | No way to call for help | Generate a dispatch summarizing all recovered context, broadcast it → **win**. |

- **#4 is the ending**: you can only send a complete dispatch after recovering
  the other three. This quietly answers "how do you win / how do checkpoints
  accumulate."

### Open questions (decide before/while building puzzles)
1. **Does the arc + mapping feel right?** (e.g. helmet → recap)
2. **Command engine:** simulated in-game terminal (portable, shareable web demo)
   vs. real CLI execution (authentic, needs a local backend) vs. hybrid.
   _Leaning simulated for a shareable demo; not yet decided._
3. **How literal is the command output?** Real-ish `entire` output vs. softened
   ship-AI flavor text.
4. **Win/lose model:** currently leaning "restore the ship's log" (collect all
   fragments, no hard lose state). Oxygen/timer and branching endings were
   other options.

### Next step
Fully design **#1 (EVA Helmet / `recap`)** end-to-end as the template, then
clone the pattern to the other three.

## Architecture / where things live

```
index.html         # canvas + HUD + stub inspection panel + loader
src/style.css      # HUD / panel / loader styling
src/main.js        # scene, camera, lighting, env (IBL), starfield, planet,
                   #   clouds, atmosphere, ring, debris, raycast hover/click,
                   #   render loop. Tuning knobs live here (light/env levels).
src/noise.js       # seedable 3D simplex + fBm (sampled on the sphere → seamless)
src/planet.js      # bakes color / bump / roughness / metalness maps + clouds.
                   #   Palette constants (DEEP/SHALLOW/SNOW, LAND_STOPS) here.
src/atmosphere.js  # fresnel limb-glow shell shader
src/ring.js        # procedural banded ring (radial-remapped UVs)
src/debris.js      # surface-anchored fragments; FRAGMENTS[] = lore + command
```

Run: `npm install` then `npm run dev` → http://localhost:5173

## History
Repo started as `signalkit` (a Python telemetry CLI), then pivoted to Planetfall
on 2026-06-05. The old code is recoverable via git history + Entire checkpoints.

aiTitle: Implement clickable islands to enter landscape view

content.text: Let me quickly check how islands and debris are currently represented, since that affects how "click into an island" would work.

This is a great direction — and it's a meaningful shift from what's there now, so let me make sure I build toward the right thing.

Here's what I'm seeing in the current code, to ground the conversation:

- **Islands today aren't real objects.** They're painted into the planet's texture maps (`planet.js` bakes the gold landmasses into the color/metalness maps). There's no "island" mesh to click yet.
- **The debris are the "elements on top" you want gone** — `debris.js` anchors 4 fragments at lat/lon points floating on the sphere surface. Those are exactly the things sitting on top of the planet.

So what you're describing is really a **two-level game**: a *planet/map view* (orbit, pick an island — basically what exists) and a brand-new *island/exploration view* (drop in, see the landscape around you, find artifacts embedded in the terrain). The artifacts move from "stuck on the globe" to "hidden in a real landscape."

That's a clean and exciting structure — and it lines up perfectly with your 4-puzzle plan: **4 islands = 4 levels**, one `entire` command each.

Before I start building, three forks genuinely change the architecture and I don't want to guess on them:

questions.question: When a player clicks an island, how should going "into" it work?

options.label: Cut to an island level
options.description: Each island is its own procedurally-generated landscape scene. The planet view fades/zooms out, the island scene fades in. Clean separation — each island is a self-contained level/puzzle. Most tractable and matches the 4-puzzle plan.
options.preview:
PLANET VIEW          ISLAND VIEW
  ___                 /\  /\___
 /   \    click      /  \/     \
| ::: |  ------->   /  terrain  \
 \___/   (fade)    /____________\

separate scene per island

options.label: Seamless dive to surface
options.description: Camera flies down and the planet's curved surface becomes the ground you stand on — one continuous world. More immersive, but much harder (terrain LOD, horizon curvature, no clean 'level' boundary).

questions.question: Once you're on an island, how does the player explore the landscape?

options.label: Orbit a landscape diorama
options.description: You rotate/zoom around a detailed island terrain (like a model on a table); artifacts sit in the terrain and you click them. Reuses the current OrbitControls, low risk, ships fast.
options.preview:
_____

  /  /\  *   \ \      around it

options.label: Walk around it (FP/TP)
options.description: WASD/pointer movement across the terrain in first- or third-person; you physically walk up to artifacts. Most immersive 'see what's around you' feel, but a much bigger build (movement, collision, camera).

questions.question: How many islands, and how do they map to puzzles?

options.label: 4 islands, 1 puzzle each
options.description: Exactly 4 named islands on the planet, each a level for one fragment/command (helmet→recap, logbook→checkpoint, panel→activity, beacon→dispatch). Tightest match to the existing plan.

options.label: More islands, 4 are 'real'
options.description: Several islands for visual richness; 4 are the playable puzzle levels and the rest are decorative/locked for now.

## Status

- Procedural planet: lavender ocean, gold **metallic** islands (PBR
  metalness + low roughness + `RoomEnvironment` IBL → shimmer), soft frost

### Not built yet ⏳
- Puzzle mechanics (panels are lore stubs only).
- The command engine (see open questions).
- Win/lose + checkpoint-collection state.
- Polish backlog: bloom on gold glints, ring shadow cast on planet, recolor
  debris halos from cyan → warm gold/white to match the palette, spread the
  islands out a bit.

## Puzzle design (PROPOSED — not yet reviewed/built)

Arc: **orient → restore → understand → escape**. Each puzzle is small: run a
command → read the recovered context → use one detail from it.

| # | Fragment | Command | What's lost | Puzzle |
|---|----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | 🪖 EVA Helmet | `recap` | "What was I doing before the crash?" | Run `recap`; read the summary of recent log entries; pick out the mission. |
| 2 | 📖 Torn Logbook | `checkpoint` | A setting got corrupted in the crash | Search checkpoints, find the last good one before the corruption, rewind to it. |
| 3 | 🖥️ Nav Panel | `activity` | "*When* did it go wrong?" | Read the activity timeline; find when the anomaly started. |
| 4 | 📡 Signal Beacon | `dispatch` | No way to call for help | Generate a dispatch summarizing all recovered context, broadcast it → **win**. |

- **#4 is the ending**: you can only send a complete dispatch after recovering
  the other three. This quietly answers "how do you win / how do checkpoints
  accumulate."

### Open questions (decide before/while building puzzles)
1. **Does the arc + mapping feel right?** (e.g. helmet → recap)
2. **Command engine:** simulated in-game terminal (portable, shareable web demo)
   vs. real CLI execution (authentic, needs a local backend) vs. hybrid.
   _Leaning simulated for a shareable demo; not yet decided._
3. **How literal is the command output?** Real-ish `entire` output vs. softened
   ship-AI flavor text.
4. **Win/lose model:** currently leaning "restore the ship's log" (collect all
   fragments, no hard lose state). Oxygen/timer and branching endings were
   other options.

### Next step
Fully design **#1 (EVA Helmet / `recap`)** end-to-end as the template, then
clone the pattern to the other three.

## Architecture / where things live

```
index.html         # canvas + HUD + stub inspection panel + loader
src/style.css      # HUD / panel / loader styling
src/main.js        # scene, camera, lighting, env (IBL), starfield, planet,

src/noise.js       # seedable 3D simplex + fBm (sampled on the sphere → seamless)
src/planet.js      # bakes color / bump / roughness / metalness maps + clouds.
                   #   Palette constants (DEEP/SHALLOW/SNOW, LAND_STOPS) here.
src/atmosphere.js  # fresnel limb-glow shell shader
src/ring.js        # procedural banded ring (radial-remapped UVs)
src/debris.js      # surface-anchored fragments; FRAGMENTS[] = lore + command
```

# Planetfall — Plan & Status

_Last updated: 2026-06-05_

## The pitch

You're a stranded astronaut. Your ship's AI has **amnesia** — it lost all its
context about what happened. To escape, you explore a 3D planet, find debris,
and each piece hides a small puzzle solved by **recovering lost context**.

The hook: reconstructing the ship's memory in-fiction **is the same act** as
recovering a real codebase's history with [Entire](https://docs.entire.io).
Each puzzle teaches a real `entire` command.

> Note: `entire explain` does **not** exist (it was an early guess). The real
> context-recovery commands are `recap`, `checkpoint`, `activity`, `dispatch`.

## Locked decisions

- **3D, web-based.** Three.js + Vite, no downloaded assets — the planet is
  generated procedurally at load.
- **Planet look (approved):** a dreamy **lavender ocean world** with a few
  **gold metallic islands** that shimmer, wrapped in a **Saturn-style ring**.
  Soft, minimal, calm.
  - NOT earthlike. NOT the glowing-magenta-"neural"-vein look (tried it; it
    looked "itchy" — busy land texture fighting bright veins. Removed.)
  - Keep lighting (sun / ambient / env) **low** — the lavender sea washes out
    white if over-lit.
- **Build order:** get the world feeling right **first** (done), then puzzles,
  then the command engine + win/lose.

## Status

### Done ✅ — the 3D world (user-approved)
- Procedural planet: lavender ocean, gold **metallic** islands (PBR

# Planetfall — Plan & Status

_Last updated: 2026-06-05_

## The pitch

You're a stranded astronaut. Your ship's AI has **amnesia** — it lost all its
context about what happened. To escape, you explore a 3D planet, find debris,
and each piece hides a small puzzle solved by **recovering lost context**.

The hook: reconstructing the ship's memory in-fiction **is the same act** as
recovering a real codebase's history with [Entire](https://docs.entire.io).
Each puzzle teaches a real `entire` command.

> Note: `entire explain` does **not** exist (it was an early guess). The real
> context-recovery commands are `recap`, `checkpoint`, `activity`, `dispatch`.

  generated procedurally at load.

  Soft, minimal, calm.

    white if over-lit.

  then the command engine + win/lose.

index.html         # canvas + HUD + stub inspection panel + loader
src/style.css      # HUD / panel / loader styling
src/main.js        # scene, camera, lighting, env (IBL), starfield, planet,

src/noise.js       # seedable 3D simplex + fBm (sampled on the sphere → seamless)
src/planet.js      # bakes color / bump / roughness / metalness maps + clouds.
                   #   Palette constants (DEEP/SHALLOW/SNOW, LAND_STOPS) here.
src/atmosphere.js  # fresnel limb-glow shell shader
src/ring.js        # procedural banded ring (radial-remapped UVs)
src/debris.js      # surface-anchored fragments; FRAGMENTS[] = lore + command
```

Run: `npm install` then `npm run dev` → http://localhost:5173

## History
Repo started as `signalkit` (a Python telemetry CLI), then pivoted to Planetfall
on 2026-06-05. The old code is recoverable via git history + Entire checkpoints.

content.text:
All tasks done. Here's what I built and verified.

## What changed

Your instinct was right — the artifacts were floating on the globe because `debris.js` just pinned them at lat/lon on the sphere surface. I restructured the game into **two views** with a crossfade between them:

**Orbit view** (`planetView.js`) — the planet you already approved, but the surface debris is gone. Instead there's **one gold "landing hotspot"** (a pulsing ring + beam) placed on a *guaranteed* patch of gold land. I added `createElevationSampler()` to `planet.js` that reuses the exact same noise field as the texture bake, so the marker always sits on visible land, never the sea. Hover highlights it; click → fade → drop in.

**Island view** (`islandView.js` + `terrain.js` + `firstPerson.js`) — a **walkable, first-person** landscape. Procedural terrain with a radial falloff so gold land rises out of a lavender sea, under a lavender-dusk sky. WASD + mouse-look (pointer lock), camera glued to the ground and clamped to the island. The 4 artifacts (reusing your `debris.js` prop builders) are embedded **in the terrain** with tall findable light-beams — walk up to one, **press E** to inspect (opens the same fragment panel), **B** to fly back to orbit.

I went **first-person** rather than third-person — strongest "see what's around me" feel, no character model needed. Easy to switch if you'd rather have a body.

## Verified

Both views render in headless Chrome (WebGL2), no console errors:
- **Orbit:** planet + ring + the gold hotspot on a landmass.
- **Island:** you're standing on gold terrain, two artifact beams ahead, control prompt at the bottom.

Try it: `npm run dev`, then drag/click the gold marker. (Pointer-lock walking needs a real browser click, so I couldn't drive the full walk-around headlessly — but the scene, terrain, and artifacts all render.)

## Honest rough edges (in PLAN.md backlog)

- The island reads a bit like **empty dunes** — it needs props/landmarks/vegetation to feel inhabited.
- The artifact **beams read pink** over the lavender sky (additive blending tints them) — want them more gold.
- Water/sky are flat placeholders.

Two things worth deciding next, when you're ready: **how the island should feel dressed** (rocks, a wrecked ship, ruins?) and whether to keep first-person or add a third-person body. Want me to do a polish pass on the island, or move on to designing the first artifact puzzle (`recap`)?

# Planetfall — Plan & Status

_Last updated: 2026-06-05_

## The pitch

You're a stranded astronaut. Your ship's AI has **amnesia** — it lost all its
context about what happened. To escape, you explore a 3D planet, find debris,
and each piece hides a small puzzle solved by **recovering lost context**.

The hook: reconstructing the ship's memory in-fiction **is the same act** as
recovering a real codebase's history with [Entire](https://docs.entire.io).
Each puzzle teaches a real `entire` command.

> Note: `entire explain` does **not** exist (it was an early guess). The real
> context-recovery commands are `recap`, `checkpoint`, `activity`, `dispatch`.

## Locked decisions

- **3D, web-based.** Three.js + Vite, no downloaded assets — the planet is
  generated procedurally at load.
- **Planet look (approved):** a dreamy **lavender ocean world** with a few
  **gold metallic islands** that shimmer, wrapped in a **Saturn-style ring**.
  Soft, minimal, calm.
  - NOT earthlike. NOT the glowing-magenta-"neural"-vein look (tried it; it
    looked "itchy" — busy land texture fighting bright veins. Removed.)
  - Keep lighting (sun / ambient / env) **low** — the lavender sea washes out
    white if over-lit.
- **Build order:** get the world feeling right **first** (done), then puzzles,
  then the command engine + win/lose.
- **Two-level structure (approved 2026-06-05):** the game is two views, not one.
  - **Orbit view** — bird's-eye planet; click a marked **island hotspot** to land.
  - **Island view** — a **walkable, first-person** landscape (WASD + mouse-look);
    artifacts are embedded **in the terrain**, not floating on the globe. Walk up
    to one and press **E** to inspect; **B** leaves back to orbit.
  - Transition is a **crossfade cut** into a dedicated island scene (not a
    seamless dive onto the sphere).
  - Starting with **one island holding all the artifacts/puzzles**; more later.

## Status

### Done ✅ — the 3D world (user-approved)
- Procedural planet: lavender ocean, gold **metallic** islands (PBR
  metalness + low roughness + `RoomEnvironment` IBL → shimmer), soft frost
  poles, drifting clouds.
- Saturn-style banded ring (tilted, two gaps, transparent).
- Soft lavender fresnel atmosphere, violet-tinted starfield.
- Orbit + zoom controls; slow auto-rotation.
- Verified rendering in headless Chrome (WebGL2), no console errors.

### Done ✅ — the two-level flow (2026-06-05)
- **Orbit view** (`planetView.js`): the planet plus one **gold landing hotspot**
  (pulsing ring + beam) placed on a guaranteed patch of gold land via a shared
  elevation sampler. Hover-highlights; click → crossfade → island.
- **Island view** (`islandView.js`): a procedural **walkable terrain** island
  (radial-falloff heightmap, gold highlands, surrounded by lavender sea),
  lavender-dusk sky, soft warm sun. **First-person** controller (`firstPerson.js`,
  PointerLockControls + WASD, glued to the terrain, clamped to the island).
- The 4 artifacts (reusing the `debris.js` prop builders) sit **in the terrain**
  with tall findable light-beams; walk within range → "Press E to inspect" →
  opens the shared fragment panel. **B** returns to orbit.
- Verified both views render in headless Chrome (WebGL2), no console errors.

### Not built yet ⏳
- Puzzle mechanics (panels are still lore stubs only).
- The command engine (see open questions).
- Win/lose + checkpoint-collection state.
- More islands (only one is built; orbit view shows a single hotspot).
- Polish backlog: bloom on gold glints, ring shadow cast on planet; island
  pass — props/landmarks so the terrain reads less like empty dunes, water
  shader, a proper skybox, third-person option if wanted; tune the beam tint
  (reads pink over the lavender sky).

## Puzzle design (PROPOSED — not yet reviewed/built)

Arc: **orient → restore → understand → escape**. Each puzzle is small: run a
command → read the recovered context → use one detail from it.

| # | Fragment | Command | What's lost | Puzzle |
|---|----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | 🪖 EVA Helmet | `recap` | "What was I doing before the crash?" | Run `recap`; read the summary of recent log entries; pick out the mission. |
| 2 | 📖 Torn Logbook | `checkpoint` | A setting got corrupted in the crash | Search checkpoints, find the last good one before the corruption, rewind to it. |
| 3 | 🖥️ Nav Panel | `activity` | "*When* did it go wrong?" | Read the activity timeline; find when the anomaly started. |
| 4 | 📡 Signal Beacon | `dispatch` | No way to call for help | Generate a dispatch summarizing all recovered context, broadcast it → **win**. |

- **#4 is the ending**: you can only send a complete dispatch after recovering
  the other three. This quietly answers "how do you win / how do checkpoints
  accumulate."

### Open questions (decide before/while building puzzles)
1. **Does the arc + mapping feel right?** (e.g. helmet → recap)
2. **Command engine:** simulated in-game terminal (portable, shareable web demo)
   vs. real CLI execution (authentic, needs a local backend) vs. hybrid.
   _Leaning simulated for a shareable demo; not yet decided._
3. **How literal is the command output?** Real-ish `entire` output vs. softened
   ship-AI flavor text.
4. **Win/lose model:** currently leaning "restore the ship's log" (collect all
   fragments, no hard lose state). Oxygen/timer and branching endings were
   other options.

### Next step
Fully design **#1 (EVA Helmet / `recap`)** end-to-end as the template, then
clone the pattern to the other three.

## Architecture / where things live

```
index.html         # canvas + HUD + crossfade overlay + FP prompt + panel + loader
src/style.css      # HUD / panel / loader / fade / crosshair / FP-prompt styling
src/main.js        # VIEW MANAGER: owns renderer + resize + shared panel + the
                   #   render loop; crossfades between the two views.
                   #   ?view=island jumps straight onto the island (dev aid).
src/planetView.js  # orbit view: scene/lighting/stars/planet/clouds/atmo/ring +
                   #   the clickable gold landing hotspot. onIslandClick callback.
src/islandView.js  # island level: sky/light, terrain+water, the artifacts, the
                   #   proximity "press E" interaction. enter()/exit()/onExit.
src/terrain.js     # procedural island heightmap mesh + water + heightAt(x,z)
src/firstPerson.js # PointerLockControls + WASD walker, glued to the terrain
src/noise.js       # seedable 3D simplex + fBm (sphere-sampled → seamless)
src/planet.js      # bakes planet maps; createElevationSampler() finds land for
                   #   the hotspot. Palette constants (DEEP/SHALLOW/SNOW) here.
src/atmosphere.js  # fresnel limb-glow shell shader (orbit view)
src/ring.js        # procedural banded ring (radial-remapped UVs)
src/debris.js      # FRAGMENTS[] (lore + command) + exported prop BUILDERS,
                   #   reused as the island artifacts. createDebris() is now
                   #   unused (kept for reference / git history).
```

Run: `npm install` then `npm run dev` → http://localhost:5173
Controls: orbit = drag/scroll, click the gold marker to land. Island = click to
capture mouse, WASD to walk, E to inspect a glowing artifact, B to leave.

## History
Repo started as `signalkit` (a Python telemetry CLI), then pivoted to Planetfall
on 2026-06-05. The old code is recoverable via git history + Entire checkpoints.

content.text:
Done. Here's what changed:

## The glow is gone — replaced with a map pin + hover outline

**Map pin (always visible):** a clean gold teardrop pin with a "LANDING SITE" label, rendered as HTML and projected onto the island's screen position each frame. No beam, no additive glow — it reads as deliberate map UI. It's a real `<button>`, so it's clickable and keyboard-focusable, and it hides itself when the island rotates to the far side of the planet.

**Hover outline (on hover):** a thin, non-glowing ring fades in flat on the surface around the landing zone, and the pin lifts slightly. Hovering either the pin *or* the island region on the globe triggers it; clicking either lands you. Verified in the screenshot — the pin sits right on a gold landmass and looks crisp.

**Island in view on load:** `enter()` now computes the island's world position and frames the camera straight at it (tipped slightly so it sits just below center), so you see it the instant the page loads instead of having to hunt by dragging. I also slowed the auto-rotation a touch so it drifts gently rather than spinning the island away.

One thing I couldn't verify headlessly: the **hover outline ring** and the pin's lift animation need a real pointer hover, which a static screenshot can't trigger. The pin position and "island faces camera" logic are confirmed working; the hover ring shares the exact same `hot` state as the pin, so if the pin highlights, the ring shows. Worth a quick look in a real browser (`npm run dev`) to confirm the ring sits nicely around *this* island's shape.

A heads-up on a tradeoff: the outline is a **circular landing-zone ring**, not a trace of the island's actual coastline — the island is painted into the planet texture, not a separate mesh, so a true coastline outline would be a much bigger job. The circle reads as a clean "landing zone." If you want it to literally hug the island's edges, that's a separate effort we can scope later.

Want to look at it live and tune the pin/ring (size, color, label text), or move on to dressing the island or designing the first puzzle?
